coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of dielectric constituents to construct ternary heterojunction structures for high-efficiency electromagnetic response
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Quan, Bin
Xu, Guoyue
Li, Daoran
Liu, Wei
Ji, Guangbin
Du, Youwei
coredata.dc:description: To satisfy the diverse requirements of low reflection and high absorption of microwave attenuation, the construction of multiple heterojunction structure is imperative. On the one hand, the impedance mismatching could be ameliorated via the addition of new component; on the other hand, the multiple interface polarizations derived from the architecture of heterojunction make for the dissipation of microwave. In this work, the ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites exhibit tremendous superiority compared with single TiO2 or RGO no matter the absorption coefficient or effective bandwidth. The maximum absorption value of the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites is −44.05dB at 14.48GHz with a low thickness of 2.0mm. In addition, the effective bandwidth (RL<−10dB) reaches 5.6GHz from 11.96 to 17.56GHz. The superior electromagnetic wave absorbing performance of the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites derived from the appropriate impedance matching as well as the multiple polarization effect. The results adequately demonstrate the accessibility of the prepared TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites as a preeminent absorber.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiple heterojunction structure
Impedance matching
Interface polarizations
TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites
Electromagnetic wave absorption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of TiO2, TiO2/RGO and TRF composites; TEM images of TiO2/RGO (b) and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 (c); (d) high resolution TEM image of the selected area in part c.
Survey scan (a), C 1s (b), Fe 2p (c) XPS spectra; (d) Raman spectrum of the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 sample.
UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO2, RGO and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3.

3D plots of RL values for paraffin-based composites: (a) TiO2, (b) RGO, (c) TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3; EM wave absorption property comparison of TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 at various thickness: (d) the maximum absorption values, (e) peak width for RL<−10dB and (f) RL<−20dB. The reflection loss values comparison between TiO2/RGO (g) and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 (h).
Frequency dependence of the (a) real and (b) imaginary parts of complex permittivity for the three as-prepared samples.
Cole-Cole plots of (a) TiO2, (b) RGO, (c) TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites with 40wt% paraffin addition.
Frequency dependence of attenuation constant (α) and impedance matching ratio (Zr ) of TiO2, RGO and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites.
Microwave absorption properties of some binary or ternary composites reported recently.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of dielectric constituents to construct ternary heterojunction structures for high-efficiency electromagnetic response

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Technology, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 211100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Technology
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Solid State Microstructures, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The obtained ternary heterojunction structures of TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites exhibit enhanced microwave absorption ability compared to the single TiO2 or RGO due to the ameliorated impedance mismatching characteristics and the multiple interface polarizations derived from the architecture of heterojunctions.

To satisfy the diverse requirements of low reflection and high absorption of microwave attenuation, the construction of multiple heterojunction structure is imperative. On the one hand, the impedance mismatching could be ameliorated via the addition of new component; on the other hand, the multiple interface polarizations derived from the architecture of heterojunction make for the dissipation of microwave. In this work, the ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites exhibit tremendous superiority compared with single TiO2 or RGO no matter the absorption coefficient or effective bandwidth. The maximum absorption value of the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites is −44.05dB at 14.48GHz with a low thickness of 2.0mm. In addition, the effective bandwidth (RL<−10dB) reaches 5.6GHz from 11.96 to 17.56GHz. The superior electromagnetic wave absorbing performance of the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites derived from the appropriate impedance matching as well as the multiple polarization effect. The results adequately demonstrate the accessibility of the prepared TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites as a preeminent absorber.

ce:section.ce:para: The widespread applications of communication technology in both military and civil fields bring severe electromagnetic (EM) pollution, not only for its interference on communication facilities and electronic devices but also for its damage on physical health [1–3]. Dielectric loss absorbers have drawn extensive attention in recent years [4,5] due to their distinct merits compared to magnetic materials, whose high density, easy oxidation and magnetic aggregation limit its application as excellent absorbers [6,7].

Among the numerous non-magnetic absorbers, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and TiO2, as typical carbonaceous material and semiconductor, have promised huge potential in the microwave absorption fields [8,9]. RGO, a new class of graphene derivatives, has been widely used as EM wave absorber due to its stable structure, light weight, large specific surface, high conductivity and easy binding ability with other materials [10,11]. Likewise, TiO2 possess the nontoxicity, low cost and long-term chemical stability, thus, it is also deemed as potential candidate of EM wave absorber [12,13].

Nonetheless, as far as an ideal EM wave absorber, neither RGO nor TiO2 is the right choice. As a matter of fact, an excellent absorber should give consideration to both impedance matching and attenuation capacity [14,15]. The strong dielectric loss capacity of RGO is derived from its high permittivity, which is exactly the root of the poor impedance matching ability of RGO according to the impedance matching conditions [16]. If the microwave cannot penetrate into the internal part of absorber, the high attenuation ability makes no sense. Oppositely, if the loss ability is too low, the incident EM wave cannot be consumed away and would reflect out of the absorber again in spite of the fine impedance matching just like TiO2. Therefore, strong reflection loss in wide frequency range cannot be achieved. To broaden the absorption bandwidth and enhance the EM absorption capacity, appropriate candidates are required to mediate the electromagnetic behaviours. Ordinary combination of two materials can hardly bring about uniform conjunction, even some undesired aggregation may occur during the reaction process.

In recent years, α-Fe203 has drawn much attention in various fields, such as sensors, catalysis, and electromagnetic wave absorption due to its stability, easy accessibility, low cost and eco-friendly nature [17,18]. Above all, hematite α-Fe203 can exactly meet the requirements in this work as an additive to improve the poor microwave absorbing performance of single material. Hence, the iron salts was pre-added in the mixture of RGO solution and TiO2 powder, the hetero-nanostructure between Fe2O3/RGO as well as Fe2O3/TiO2 can be obtained via the hydrothermal treatment. On the one hand, the integral dielectric constant of TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites can be adjusted through the introduction of Fe2O3. Furthermore, the multiple interfaces from the heterojunctions can generate a mass of polarization, which benefits the loss of EM wave. The obtained TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites exhibit remarkable EM wave absorption capacity of −44.05dB at 14.48GHz with a low thickness of 2.0mm, meanwhile the maximal bandwidth below −10dB reaches 5.6GHz from 11.96 to 17.56GHz, which is much better than RGO or TiO2 alone. This work effectively solves the issues of pure RGO or TiO2 as EM wave absorber. In addition, the artificially designed heterostructures with target functionalities can also be imitated in other fields besides microwave absorption.

The TiO2 nanosheets were synthesized by a typical solvothermal method. In detail, 25mL of Ti(OBu)4 and 3mL HF were added into distilled water with continuous stirring. Then the uniform mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 24h. After cooled to room temperature, the white sample was obtained through centrifugation and immediately washed with distilled water and ethyl alcohol.

The ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites were obtained via a simple hydrothermal approach. Briefly, 30mL GO solution was added to a mixture of distilled water (30mL) and ethanol (15mL) under 2h of ultrasonic treatment. Then 75mg as-prepared TiO2 and 200mg FeCl3·6H2O were added the above mentioned solution in sequence. The obtained solution was heated at 150°C for 5h. The final products were separated via centrifugation, washed alternately for several times with ethyl alcohol and distilled water, and dried at 60°C for 24h in a vacuum. For the convenience to discuss, the prepared TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 samples are denoted as TRF.

The crystal structure of samples were determined by a Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer with a 2θ range from 10° to 90° at 40mA and 40kV using CuKα radiation. Microscopic characterization was carried out using transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JSM-2010) operated at a 3.0kV of acceleration voltage. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw InVia). The XPS spectrum information was obtained by a PHI 5000 VersaProbe systems with an AlKα X-ray source at 150W. UV–Vis absorption spectra was determined using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). The S parameters of S11 and S21 were measured at 2–18GHz using an Agilent PNA N5244A vector network analyzer through a coaxial-line method, in which the sample for microwave measurement was prepared by homogeneously mixing paraffin wax with 60wt% products and then pressing into toroidal shaped samples of 3.04mm in inner diameter, 7.00mm in outer diameter.

From Fig.1 b, it can be clearly seen that the as-prepared TiO2 nanosheets with an average dimension of 60nm (length)∗40nm (width) are deposited on the surface of RGO well. Generally, uniform conjunction cannot be obtained via simple combination, also some undesired aggregation may occur during the reaction process. Here, an iron salt (III) was pre-added to the mixture with GO solution and TiO2 powder. The absorbed iron ions onto the surface of GO and TiO2 can transform into dispersed Fe2O3 nanoparticles (NPs) via the further hydrothermal treatment. Fig.1b exhibits the TEM image of TiO2/RGO composites, where TiO2 are scattered onto the RGO surface. While, the ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites are well constructed via the simple method, as shown in Fig.1c. Further structure analysis was also conducted through high-resolution TEM measurement (Fig.1d). The clear fringe space of 0.35nm and 0.25nm correspond to the (101) lattice of anatase titanium dioxide and the (110) lattice of hematite ferric oxide, respectively. In addition, the unsharp twisty fringe is corresponding to lattice plane of RGO, which is derived from the disorderedly stacked rGO and the damage of the rGO layered structures with the introduction of Ti4+ and Fe3+ ions [19].

In addition, X-ray diffraction patterns are used to determine the crystal structure and chemical composition of the obtained samples, as shown in Fig.1a. The characteristic diffraction peak of RGO at 24.4° can be well matched to the graphitic (002) planes, which agrees with our previous work [11]. As for TiO2, all peaks can correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204) planes of anatase titanium dioxide (JCPDS # 99-0008). The obtained ternary composites basically retain the crystal structure of TiO2, and the characteristic peaks at 33.1° as well as 35.6° can be assigned to the (104) and (110) plane of hexagonal-phase Fe2O3. Notably, the (101) diffraction peak of TiO2 and the (104) diffraction peak of Fe2O3 match well with the clear lattice fringes marked in the TEM image. It is worth mentioning that the peak of RGO at about 24.4° cannot be found in the sample, which accords with the analysis in Fig.1d.

The chemical state of the elements in the composites was demonstrated with XPS. The survey scan profile (Fig.2 a) reveals the chemical element composition of Ti, Fe, O and C in the composites. C 1s spectrums with four different peaks are obtained, as shown in Fig.2b. The peaks centered at 284.64, 286.08, 287.03, 289.28eV are attributed to the CC/CC, CO or alkoxy, CO and OCO groups of reduced graphene oxide, respectively. Particularly, the existence of the oxygen-containing functional groups like CO and OCO exhibits the reduction degree of GO is not complete. Fe 2p spectra is presented in Fig.2c, the Fe 2p3/2 at 711.1eV and Fe 2p1/2 at 724.6eV as well we the distinct satellite peak (indicated by arrow) at around 719.02eV correspond to the generation of Fe2O3. From the Raman spectra (Fig.2d), two typical peaks of RGO at 1345 and 1593cm−1 can be assigned to the D band (disorder or defects in graphene) and G band (caused by the in-plane vibrations), respectively [20–22]. The intensity ratio of (ID/IG) can reflect the degree of disorders and defects of the graphitic structures. The calculated ID/IG value of 1.05 reveals that the obtained RGO keeps the regular arrangement just in short range, which is corresponding to the TEM, XRD and XPS results. Moreover, the stretching vibration peaks of TiO2 and Fe2O3 at relevant locations (marked in near the peaks) are also observed. Taken together, it is conclusively demonstrated that the ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites have been successfully obtained.

In order to illustrate the accomplishment of the construction of ternary heterojunction, the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance of TiO2, RGO and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 were carried out. In fact, the variation of photoabsorption primarily stems from the change of electron transition and migration, which is derived from the transformation of energy band with the construction of heterojunction. As shown in Fig. 3 , the three samples of TiO2, RGO and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 exhibit diverse degrees of photoresponse region from ultraviolet to infrared light, and the band gap of TiO2, RGO and TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 can be calculated to be 3.20, 3.37 and 3.35eV, respectively, by the formula λ =1239.8/Eg
[23]. All the phenomena indicate that the ternary heterojunction have been successfully built after the hydrothermal process. It stands to reason that the there are two sides of effect for the formation of heterojunction on the effective absorption of EM wave. (i) The generation of energy level gradient and energy barrier derived from the construction of heterojunction can accelerate or suppress the flow of electron, which have direct relationship with the electric conductivity/resistivity [24]. (ii) There exists different electron distributions status between the multiple-interfaces in the heterojunction structures, which could cause abundant accumulation of electron at the interfaces [25]. Hence, it will be bound to bring about drastic interface polarization in this case. Moreover, the electric conductivity/resistivity or the multiple interface polarization would make an immediate impact on the complex permittivity.

In general, part of the incident EM wave reflects back to the air directly when the EM wave reaches the surface of metal-based absorbers, while the remainder enters into the inner, whose intensity may be weaken during the transmission. Once the EM wave comes to the metal layer, all of them reflect back to the absorbers. When the EM wave returns to the interface of absorber and air, some of it reflects back to the absorber and the others penetrate into the air medium. The course mentioned above would repeat over and over until the inner EM wave is completely consumed [26]. A high-efficiency absorber desires as less reflected EM wave as possible, and the microwave absorption properties are calculated by the transmission line theory, which can be summarized as the following equations:
(1)
RL = 20 log | (
Z
in
-
Z
0
) / (
Z
in
+
Z
0
) |
(2)
Z
in
=
Z
0
μ
r
ε
r
tanh
j
2 π fd
c
μ
r
ε
r
where Z 0 is the impedance of free space, Zin is the input characteristic impedance, c is the velocity of light, d is the absorber thickness, μr and εr are complex permeability and complex permittivity values. Both TiO2 and RGO exhibit poor EM wave absorbing performance (RL<−10dB) from the thickness of 1–5mm at 2–18GHz. Obviously, the ternary composites show observably enhanced EM wave absorbing abilities than pure TiO2 and RGO, as shown in Fig.4 a–c. The maximum RL value of the ternary composites is −44.05dB at 14.48GHz with a low thickness of 2.0mm. Moreover, there is an especial phenomenon that the surface of RL is curved and wrinkled. In order to make it clear, the metabolic transmission line formula is used, where γ represents the propagation constant and ρ is the semi-infinite reflection coefficient [17]:
(3)
γ = j ω
μ
r
ε
r
= α + j β
(4)
ρ =
μ
r
ε
r
- 1
μ
r
ε
r
+ 1
= | ρ |
e
j θ
where Eqs. (3) and (4) are taken into Eqs. (1) and (2), the following formula can be obtained:
(5)
RL = 101 g
| ρ
|
2
+
e
- 4 α d
- 2 | ρ |
e
- θ - 2 α d
cos ( θ + 2 α d )
1 + ρ
|
2
e
- 4 α d
- 12 | ρ |
e
θ - 2 α d
cos ( θ - 2 α d )

The β is the function of frequency (f), therefore the angle of cosine (θ ±2αd) changes with the variation of f. In addition, the matching thickness d, a part of angle of cosine (θ ±2αd), directly affects the cosine value. As seen from Eq. (5), the RL is linked closely with the cosine part, the surface of RL would wrinkle and curve as the alternant variation of d and f in the 3D space model. To further investigate the microwave absorption performance of TRF, the thickness dependence of the maximum absorption values, effective peak width for RL<−10dB and RL<−20dB are exhibited in Fig.4d–f, respectively. It can be concluded from Fig.4a that the best microwave absorption performance appears at the matching thickness of 2.0mm and the maximum RL value decreases with the increase of thickness. As seen in Fig.4b, the effective bandwidth of RL<−10dB increases from 1.5 to 2.0mm then it decreases with the enhancement of matching thickness. The maximal bandwidth (at 2.0mm) below −10dB reaches 5.6GHz (11.96–17.56GHz), which is much larger than the reported works [27,28]. Also, the final samples also exhibits a certain bandwidth of RL<−20dB, and the peak width at 2.0mm is as width as 1.56GHz. Furthermore, in order to further demonstrate the effect of the Fe2O3 component on the enhanced microwave absorption performance, a comparison of microwave reflection loss maps between TiO2/RGO and TRF has been shown in Fig.4g and h. Obviously, the ternary composites exhibit much elevated EM wave absorbing ability, indicating the necessity of introducing Fe2O3 component. As a whole, the ternary composites with multiple heterojunction show distinct superiority in microwave absorption than TiO2, RGO or TiO2/RGO samples, meanwhile the best performance appears at the matching thickness of 2.0mm, whose maximum absorbing value and effective bandwidth are obviously better than the properties at other thickness. It is worth mentioning that the prepared ternary composites exhibit better EM wave absorption abilities compared to some reported binary [29–32] or ternary [33–35] absorbers, as shown in Table 1 , in consideration of the microwave absorption capacity, effective bandwidth as well as the matching thickness.

In view of the nonmagnetic characteristics of TiO2, RGO and the TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites, only the frequency dependence complex permittivity (εr
=
ε′
−
jε″) of the sample/paraffin composites were measured in 2–18GHz, as seen in Fig. 5 . The real permittivity (ε′) represents the storage capacity of EM energy, while the imaginary permittivity (ε″) symbolizes the dissipation capacity of EM energy [36,37]. For TiO2 nanosheets, the value of both ε′ and ε″ are relative low. Generally, the value of complex permeability is much lower than the value of complex permittivity, especially for the nonmagnetic materials in this work, therefore, the low complex permittivity can bring about nice impedance matching according to the impedance matching conditions:
(6)
Z
in
=
Z
0
(
μ
r
/
ε
2
)
1 / 2

However, the imaginary part of TiO2 is so low (almost no attenuation capacity) that the EM waves entering into the inner space would be reflected out of the absorber at last due to feeble dissipation occurring during the transmission procedure. Conversely, RGO keeps its customary high dielectric properties, whatever the real or the imaginary part of permittivity. On the basis of free-electron theory [38] as well as the skin effect formula [11]:
(7)
ε
″
≈ 1 / π
ε
o
ρ f
(8)
δ = 1 /
π f μ σ
where ρ represents the electrical resistivity, σ represents the electrical conductivity and δ is the skin depth. The high electrical conductivity of RGO can result in strong dielectric dissipation, which is in favour of the enhancement of microwave absorption. Whereas high conductivity will cause less skin depth, ultimately bring about the undesired reflection of the incident EM waves. The strong attenuation ability gets useless when little microwave enters into the inner of absorber. Hence, the EM parameters of TiO2 and RGO explain the reasons of their bad EM absorption capacity shown in Fig. 4. For the multiphase mixture, its permittivity lies in a suitable section, which can be elaborated with the commonly used logarithmic law of mixing, as shown in Eq. (8) [16]:
(9)
ln
ε
′
=
V
1
ln
ε
1
+
V
2
ln
ε
2
+
V
3
ln
ε
3
where V 1, V 1, and V 1 are volume fraction of TiO2, RGO and Fe2O3, respectively; ε 1, ε 2, and ε 3 are the real parts of complex permittivity of TiO2, RGO and Fe2O3, respectively. All the three substances possess different dielectric constant. With the variation of addition in the ternary composites, the comprehensive ε′ value can be adjusted to a proper level. In addition, the ε″ keep the similar tend like the ε′, which can be illustrated by the following function, namely [39]:
(10)
ε
′
=
ε
″
/ 2 π τ f +
ε
∞

It is noted that the complex permittivity of RGO and the final samples decreases with the increased frequency and reveals distinct frequency dispersion effect. Based on the Debye theory [40], the complex permittivity can be expressed as:
(11)
ε
′
=
ε
∞
+
ε

ε

(12)

ω τ +
σ
ω
ε
0
where εs
, ε∞ and τ are the static dielectric constant, the infinite frequency dielectric constant and relaxation time, respectively. The dipoles in the absorber can not follow the exchange of applied electric field with the frequency increasing. Accompanying with the augment of relaxation as well as the extension of relaxation time, the complex permittivity begins to decrease generates typical frequency dispersion behaviours.

In general, polarization process can modify the dielectric property and enhance the EM waves absorbing effectively. Besides the intrinsic electric dipole polarization/rotation, the multiple interface polarization has also been considered as significant factor that is responsible for the enhanced EM wave absorption. Here, the Core-Core curves of TiO2, RGO and TRF composites are shown in Fig.6 a–c to make a comparison of the relaxation process derived from the interface polarization of the ternary heterojunction. By eliminating ωτ and subtracting the contribution of σ in Eqs. (10) and (11), the relationship of ε′ and ε″ can be expressed in the following form:
(13)
ε
′
-
ε
s
+
ε
∞
2
2
+
ε
″
-
σ
ω
ε
0
2
= (
ε
′
-
ε
s
-
ε
∞
2
2

It is corresponding to a circle centered at ((εs
+ ε∞ )/2, 0), which denotes the Debye relaxation process. It can be observed that compared with TiO2, RGO there exists more individual semicircles in the Core-Core curves, indicating the more Debye relaxation process resulted from the multiple interface polarization in the ternary heterojunction.

As a matter of fact, there are two critical factors for an excellent absorber. One is the impedance match, desiring the values of permittivity and permeability to be equality; and the other is the energy conservation, asking for strong EM propagation loss in the absorber interior. To further explore the deeper EM wave absorbing mechanism hidden inside the EM parameters, the frequency dependence of attenuation constant (α) and impedance matching ratio (Zr ) of TiO2, RGO and TRF composites at 2–18GHz were calculated, as shown in Fig. 7 . Here the impedance matching ratio value proposed by Lv et al. [41] are used to make clear the degree of impedance matching of the three samples, which can be denoted as the following:
(14)
Z
r
= Z /
Z
0
(15)
Z =
Z
0
μ
r
/
ε
r
where Z is the impedance value of the absorber, Zr is the impedance matching ratio value. The best impedance matching can be obtained when εr equals to μr , that is, if Zr
=1, all the incident EM wave can enter into the inner of the absorber. In the real world situation, the complete impedance matching is hard to obtain, therefore, the higher impedance matching value near Zr
=1 is desired. Furthermore, the strong dissipation ability is also required for an efficient absorption. According to the transmission line theory, the integral attenuation loss ability of absorber can be demonstrated by the attenuation constant (α), as can be expressed:
(16)
α =
2
π f
c
×
(
μ
″
ε
″
=
μ
′
ε
′
) +
(
μ
″
ε
″
=
μ
′
ε
′
)
2
(
μ
′
ε
″
=
μ
″
ε
′
)
2
where c denotes the velocity of light. The higher the value of μ″ and ε″ is, the higher attenuation constant α can be gained. One can find from Fig. 7 that the integral tend of attenuation constant is αRGO
> αTRF
> α TiO2 and the overall tendency of impedance matching ratio value is ZTiO2 >ZTRF >ZRGO in all frequency ranges. RGO owns the worst impedance matching property but its attenuation capacity is the best in consequence of its comparatively high complex permittivity and conductivity, while TiO2 possesses the optimal impedance matching character and but its attenuation ability is the poorest due to the relatively low complex permittivity, which are corresponding to the results of EM parameters in Fig. 5. It is important that both α and Z value of TRF are at the middle level of the three samples. It is not difficult to conclude that the point why the obtained samples possess the best microwave absorption capacity is its compatibility for impedance matching and energy conservation. Hence, enough EM wave would be introduced into the absorber, and strong attenuation ability was achieved. A proper balance between them is a meaningful issue to be explored.

In regard to EM wave absorption, there are two kinds of primary forms. Besides the commonly mentioned microwave attenuation, which is based on the proper impendence matching; the other is called the “geometrical effect” [42], which is derived from the mutual interference of EM waves. In particular, if the absorber thickness (tm ) at the peak frequency (fm ) meets the following equation:
(17)
t
m
=
nc
4
f
m
|
μ
r
| |
ε
r
|
n = 1 , 3 , 5 , . . .
where |μr | and |εr | signifies the moduli of μr and εr , the incident and reflected microwaves in the absorber are out of phase by 180°, giving rise to an extinction of each other at the air/absorber interfaces. To verify whether there exists the interference loss in the obtained absorber, the simulations of absorber thickness at the minimum RL value versus peak frequency for the sample under λ/4 conditions [43,44], as seen in Fig. 8 . The violet dots denote the experimental matching thickness (t exp m) at the minimum RL values frequency, and the black curve indicates the simulation thickness (t fit m) using the quarter wavelength principle at the same frequency range of 2–18GHz. Obviously, the value of t exp m at the given thickness is in accord with the simulation t fit m. Therefore, the peak frequency of the optimal RL value at each thickness can correspond to the geometrical effect, that is, the interference dissipation plays an important role in the EM wave attenuation process. The distinguished EM wave absorption capacity results from the compatibility of impedance matching characteristics and attenuation loss ability. Moreover, interference hardening loss is another significant dissipation factor other than the commonly mentioned dielectric and magnetic loss. The λ/4 principle can be used as an effective method to provide key guide when designing the thickness of an ideal microwave absorber.

In summary, the multiple heterojunction structures have been built via introducing iron oxide into the ternary TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites system. The reduced surface reflection and enhanced absorbing capacity mainly result from the following two points: one is amendatory impedance matching due to the addition of Fe2O3 ingredient, the other is the multiple interface polarizations for the construction of heterojunctions. The obtained TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites exhibit the excellent microwave absorbing ability. Its maximum reflection loss is −44.05dB at 14.48GHz with a low thickness of 2.0mm and the effective bandwidth below −10dB reaches 5.6GHz, covering from 11.96GHz to 17.56GHz. This work not only effectively extends the electromagnetic wave absorption performance of pure RGO or TiO2, but also proves the feasibility of TiO2/RGO/Fe2O3 composites as a distinguished electromagnetic wave absorber.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No.: 11575085), the Qing Lan Project, Six talent peaks project in Jiangsu Province (No.: XCL-035) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: B. Quan, X.H. Liang, G.Y. Xu, Y. Cheng, Y.N. Zhang, W. Liu, G.B. Ji, Y.W. Du. New J. Chem. http://dx.doi.org/10.1039/C6NJ03052A.
